q: What is the Databricks Declarative Automation Bundle (DAB) CI/CD workflow for ML pipelines? Describe the full promote-to-prod pattern.
a: A complete DAB CI/CD workflow for ML pipelines: (1) Development — data scientists work in the dev workspace, define training pipelines as Lakeflow Jobs in databricks.yml, commit changes to a feature branch. (2) Pull Request / CI — a CI pipeline (GitHub Actions, Azure DevOps) runs databricks bundle validate to check YAML syntax, then databricks bundle deploy -t staging followed by databricks bundle run -t staging to execute integration tests. (3) Staging validation — the bundle deploys all resources to staging workspace (experiments, jobs, endpoints) and runs the full training pipeline against a sample dataset; results are validated against thresholds. (4) Merge to main — after PR approval, the main branch triggers a CD pipeline that runs databricks bundle deploy -t prod, which provisions or updates all prod resources declared in the bundle. (5) Model promotion — the CD job invokes the training Lakeflow Job in prod; after training, a model validation task compares the new model to the Champion and promotes via MlflowClient.set_registered_model_alias() if quality gates pass. Key benefit: all infrastructure changes (endpoint config, cluster policies, experiment paths) are code-reviewed in PRs before reaching production, eliminating manual UI-based changes that are hard to audit.
fj: Model promotion from staging to production requires manual intervention in the Databricks UI — it cannot be automated through CI/CD pipelines.
fs: Model promotion is fully automatable: the Databricks SDK's MlflowClient.set_registered_model_alias() call can be executed from a Lakeflow Job task in the CI/CD pipeline, which promotes the model alias to Champion if quality gates pass. The entire flow — deploy, train, evaluate, promote — can run without human intervention.

q: How do you use pandas UDFs (Scalar and Iterator variants) for batch feature engineering and inference at Spark scale? How do they differ from applyInPandas?
a: Pandas UDFs (also called vectorized UDFs) use Apache Arrow for efficient columnar data transfer between JVM and Python, making them significantly faster than row-level Python UDFs. Three variants: (1) Scalar pandas UDF (SCALAR type): takes one or more pandas Series as input, returns a pandas Series. Applied column-by-column via withColumn(). Best for: per-row transformations using pandas/numpy/sklearn (feature scaling, text encoding). (2) Iterator of Series pandas UDF (SCALAR_ITER): wraps the SCALAR pattern but receives an iterator of pandas Series batches — allows loading a model once per partition rather than per row. Critical pattern for ML inference: load the model in the iterator function body before the loop, then apply it to each batch. (3) applyInPandas: groupBy().applyInPandas() processes a complete group as a pandas DataFrame — input and output are DataFrames, not Series. Use for: group-level computations that need access to all rows in a group simultaneously (group-level model training, cross-validation per group). Key difference from pandas UDFs: pandas UDFs operate row-by-row or batch-by-batch on individual columns; applyInPandas operates on a complete grouped DataFrame with full row context. For ML inference, prefer Iterator pandas UDF to amortize model loading cost across a partition.
fj: Pandas UDFs and applyInPandas are the same API — applyInPandas is just the newer name for pandas UDFs.
fs: They are distinct APIs with different input/output contracts. Pandas UDFs operate on Series (columns) and are applied via withColumn() or select(). applyInPandas requires a groupBy() and operates on a full grouped DataFrame. They serve different use cases: pandas UDFs for per-row or per-batch column transformations, applyInPandas for group-level computations requiring full row context.

q: How does Databricks Lakehouse Monitoring handle snapshot tables? What use cases and monitoring features apply?
a: Snapshot profile in Lakehouse Monitoring is designed for tables that represent a point-in-time state — not append-only event streams, but the current snapshot of a dataset (e.g., current customer attributes, a daily batch ML output table, feature table values at a given time). When you create a snapshot monitor, Databricks computes data quality metrics for the entire table at each refresh: null rates, distinct counts, mean, standard deviation, and histogram distributions per column. There is no temporal windowing — the full table is treated as a single snapshot to compare against the baseline. Drift detection: on each refresh, the snapshot's distributions are compared to the baseline reference table using Chi-squared (categorical) and Wasserstein/KS (numerical) tests, with results written to the drift_metrics output table. Use cases: (1) monitoring a daily feature engineering output for unexpected distribution shifts before it is used for training; (2) tracking changes in a scoring table between weekly runs; (3) detecting schema changes (new nulls, unexpected values) in a silver-layer Delta table. The key limitation versus time series profiles: snapshot monitors cannot compute windowed trends — they only provide 'latest snapshot vs. baseline' comparison, not temporal trend analysis.
fj: Snapshot monitors in Lakehouse Monitoring are used for real-time streaming tables — they continuously monitor each arriving micro-batch.
fs: Snapshot profiles are for bounded, non-streaming tables evaluated at refresh time. They compute metrics on the table's current state at each scheduled refresh, not on streaming micro-batches. For streaming or timestamped data, use the time series profile which supports window-based temporal analysis.

q: How do you implement a champion-challenger A/B test at the model serving layer and measure statistical significance of outcome differences?
a: A champion-challenger A/B test via Model Serving traffic splitting: (1) Deploy both Champion (alias v3) and Challenger (alias v4) to the same endpoint with a 50/50 or 80/20 split. (2) Ensure the response includes model_version metadata so each prediction is tagged with which model served it. (3) Log all predictions to an inference table (input features, model_version, prediction, and optionally user_id for later outcome join). (4) After a defined collection period, join outcomes (conversions, actual labels) to the inference table. (5) Statistical test: for conversion rate differences, use a two-proportion z-test or chi-squared test; for continuous outcomes (revenue, RMSE), use Mann-Whitney U or Welch's t-test. Minimum detectable effect size: compute required sample N before starting the test using power analysis (scipy.stats.power or statsmodels). Stop criteria: either reach N samples or reach a pre-defined evaluation period — avoid peeking at results early (multiple comparison inflation). In Databricks: run the statistical analysis as a SQL query on the inference table joined with outcomes, or as a PySpark notebook. If the Challenger is statistically significantly better (p < 0.05) and practically significant (effect > threshold), promote Challenger to Champion via MlflowClient.set_registered_model_alias().
fj: You can determine which model is better by comparing their offline validation metrics from the training run — there is no need to run a live A/B test.
fs: Offline validation metrics compute performance on historical held-out data, which may not reflect live production behavior (different user cohorts, feature drift, or serving latency effects). A/B tests measure real business outcomes on live traffic, which is the ground truth for production model quality. Models that look identical offline can differ significantly in live performance.

code.label: Statistical significance test for A/B model comparison
lines: JOIN ml.prod.outcomes o ON p.user_id = o.user_id

q: How does Databricks MLflow model versioning and aliasing work in Unity Catalog? What replaces the old Staging/Production stage workflow?
a: In the Unity Catalog Model Registry, model versions no longer have fixed lifecycle stages (None/Staging/Production/Archived) as in the legacy Workspace Model Registry. Instead, aliases provide a flexible, user-defined naming system for referencing specific versions. An alias is a mutable named pointer to a specific version: you might define Champion (production model), Challenger (candidate under evaluation), and Baseline (minimum performance benchmark). Setting an alias: MlflowClient().set_registered_model_alias(name='ml.prod.churn_model', alias='Champion', version='5'). Retrieving by alias: mlflow.pyfunc.load_model('models:/ml.prod.churn_model@Champion'). Model Serving endpoints can reference a specific version number or a registered model alias. Benefits of aliases over stages: (1) you can have multiple simultaneously valid aliases (Champion, Challenger, Baseline all active at once), (2) aliases are arbitrary strings — not limited to a fixed stage enum, (3) promotion is atomic — reassigning Champion alias to a new version is a single API call. Migration from legacy: replace references to stage='Production' with alias='Champion' in your codebase. The legacy Workspace Model Registry still supports stages, but all new models registered in Unity Catalog should use the alias pattern.
fj: In Unity Catalog, you transition model versions between Staging and Production stages just like in the legacy Workspace Model Registry.
fs: Unity Catalog Model Registry removed the fixed stage system. There are no Staging, Production, or Archived stages in UC. Instead, aliases are free-form named pointers to versions. This is a deliberate design change to support more flexible workflows — you define the lifecycle labels that match your team's deployment process rather than fitting into a fixed four-stage enum.

q: How do you log and compare MLflow SparkML models across multiple runs to identify the best model programmatically? What APIs support this?
a: After running multiple experiments (CrossValidator folds, Optuna trials, or manual runs), use mlflow.search_runs() to programmatically query and compare results. Key API: mlflow.search_runs(experiment_ids=['123'], filter_string='metrics.val_auc > 0.80', order_by=['metrics.val_auc DESC'], max_results=10) returns a pandas DataFrame of run metadata, parameters, and metrics. For nested runs (child trials), filter by tags.mlflow.parentRunId = '<parent_run_id>' to scope the search to one experiment sweep. Once you identify the best run by run_id, retrieve its model URI: f'runs:/{best_run_id}/model' and load or register it. For SparkML specifically, use mlflow.spark.log_model(spark_model, artifact_path, registered_model_name=...) to save a SparkML PipelineModel in the MLflow format — this preserves the full pipeline including all transformer stages. The Databricks Experiments UI provides a visual comparison table and parallel coordinates plot for metric/parameter relationships, useful for identifying which hyperparameter regions perform best. Programmatic best-model selection is critical in automated retraining pipelines where a human is not reviewing results.
fj: You must manually browse the Experiments UI to find the best run — there is no API to query runs by metric and retrieve the best one.
fs: mlflow.search_runs() is a full-featured programmatic API that returns a pandas DataFrame with all run metadata, parameters, and metrics. You can sort, filter, and select runs entirely in code, enabling automated champion selection in CI/CD pipelines without any human review of the Experiments UI.

q: How do you implement feature slicing in Lakehouse Monitoring to detect performance disparities across demographic or business segments?
a: Feature slicing in Lakehouse Monitoring computes all metrics (drift scores, data quality stats, model performance) separately for defined subgroups of data, enabling detection of differential performance across segments that might be hidden in aggregate metrics. Configuration: pass slicing_exprs as a list of column names or SQL expressions to lm.create_monitor(). Examples: slicing_exprs=['region', 'product_tier', 'CASE WHEN age < 30 THEN "young" WHEN age < 60 THEN "mid" ELSE "senior" END']. Lakehouse Monitoring evaluates all metrics for each slice value, writing them to the profile_metrics table with an additional slice_key and slice_value column. Analysis: query SELECT slice_key, slice_value, metric_name, metric_value FROM ml.monitoring.churn_predictions_profile_metrics WHERE slice_key IS NOT NULL ORDER BY slice_key, slice_value to compare metrics across segments. Use cases: (1) Fairness auditing — detect if model accuracy is significantly lower for a protected demographic group. (2) Business performance analysis — find if churn prediction accuracy varies by region or product tier. (3) Data quality monitoring by segment — identify if null rates are higher in specific market segments. If a slice shows consistently worse performance, it may require a dedicated model, additional training data for that segment, or feature engineering targeted at that group.
fj: Lakehouse Monitoring computes only aggregate metrics across the full table — per-segment analysis requires writing custom SQL queries on the inference table.
fs: slicing_exprs is a built-in parameter of lm.create_monitor() that automatically computes all standard metrics (drift, quality, performance) per segment and writes them to the output profile_metrics table. Custom SQL is only needed for custom metrics beyond the built-in set — standard sliced monitoring requires no custom queries.

code.label: Lakehouse Monitoring with feature slicing
lines: GROUP BY 1, 2 ORDER BY avg_accuracy ASC
